data.title: Ulta Is Well-Positioned In a Difficult Market — Market Talk
data.description: Ulta is well-positioned in a challenging environment, say Barclays analysts, who upgrade the stock to overweight. Ulta, which has a dominant place in the beauty industry, has far less tariff exposure than other beauty companies with only 1% of its merchandise in 2024 directly imported. And while broader consumer spending is uncertain, Ulta's core customer will "continue to over-index wallet share toward beauty, health and wellness categories," the analysts say. The company's margins should also see a boost from a renewed focus on optimizing distribution points through slowing expansion of its own stores and ending its partnership with Target. The company will report its 2Q earnings next week. Shares rise 2.2%. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Moderna's Updated Covid-19 Vaccine Gets Canadian Go Ahead for Fall Vaccination Season
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Moderna's updated Covid-19 vaccine has been approved by Canada's health department, putting it on track to roll out for the coming vaccine season.

The biotech company said Friday the latest version of its Covid-19 mRNA vaccine, Spikevax, has been authorized by Health Canada. And for the first time, all pre-filled syringe doses of the vaccine will be manufactured domestically at Moderna's new facility outside Montreal and completed by Novocol Pharma in Cambridge, Ontario.

Moderna said all Canadian-made doses are expected to be available this fall for the 2025-2026 vaccination season.

Pfizer and partner BioNTech earlier in the week said they had received Health Canada approval for their updated Comirnaty Covid-19 vaccine, which also will be available in pharmacies across Canada in the fall.

People who meet provincially set criteria will receive the vaccines free of charge in most Canadian provinces. The companies said they are working with private insurers and payers to streamline access and reimbursement for individuals not covered under public programs.

Moderna's updated vaccine targeting the SARS-CoV-2 LP.8.1 variant of Covid-19 has already been granted approval by regulators in Europe, Japan, Switzerland and other countries.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: US Stocks Surge After Powell Comments
data.description:
US stocks surged on Friday, trimming most of the losses from the week after remarks from Federal Reserve Chairman Powell in his Jackson Hole strengthened bets of an incoming rate cut.

The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100 jumped 1.3% to approach their records from last week, while the Dow soared 700 points.

The Chairman stated that upside risks to inflation have diminished that employment risks are on the downside, noting that the shifting balance of risks may warrant an adjustment to policy.

Even though Powell balanced the rhetoric with warnings that inflation expectations could become unanchored, the remarks drove markets to gain certainty of an incoming rate cut next month and pile on bets of more rate cuts in the fourth quarter.

Tech shares underperformed core sectors as markets on more unease on the high valuations of companies in the artificial intelligence space.

Nvidia was around the flatline ahead of its earnings next week, seen as a global indicator for AI demand.

data.description:

data.title: VTEX Is Maintained at Buy by UBS
Roblox Gets an Upgrade in a Rough Month. The Stock Is Rising. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

Roblox was rising Friday after the gaming platform received an upgrade from one Wall Street analyst during an otherwise trying month.

Shweta Khajuria of Wolfe Research upgraded Roblox to Outperform, from Peer Perform and set a price target of $150 in a research note Friday. The company, Khajuria argued, can benefit from a genuine content flywheel and still has a large addressable market to penetrate.

Shares were rising 2.3% to $117.50 on Friday.

Roblox has had a banner year, with its stock roughly doubling in 2025. But August has been a rough month after the State of Louisiana sued the company, alleging Roblox has allowed child predators to abuse its platform, whose users are mostly under 18.

The stock has dropped about 16% since its all-time closing high on July 31, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Wolfe's Khajuria acknowledged the case in Louisiana as a potential downside risk for the company, but argued that Roblox has made progress improving its platform's safety and that its fundamentals warranted an upgrade.

"We believe Roblox has a long runway for durable growth from low penetration in an attainable, large gaming [market]," Khajuria wrote. "Roblox is one of the fastest growing businesses in our coverage, and we see potential for meaningful margin expansion."

One reason for the bullish outlook is the platform's flywheel, Wolfe said. The company's use of artificial intelligence has accelerated content velocity among the platform's creators, resulting in more and higher-quality experiences that drive user engagement and spending.

In June, 28 of the top 200 experiences by total spend had been launched in the last year, up from 21 in June 2024, Khajuria pointed out.

"We expect to see a higher number of top hits emerge more frequently driven by improvements in search discovery and faster content velocity," she wrote.

Pricing optimizations can also keep the flywheel spinning. Roblox has rolled out regional pricing that adjusts prices based on location, differential pricing that gives users various tiered options, and a tool for creators to recommend optimal prices for in-game items. These features can all boost bookings and earnings, Khajuria argued.

Advertising, still in its nascent stages on Roblox, should also drive growth, she said. As the company continues to roll out video ads, Wolfe estimates an additional $300 million in gross bookings in 2026 and $650 million in 2027, which would represent a 7% uplift in total bookings.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

data.title: US banks rise as Fed chair signals possible rate cut in September
data.description:
** Economy-sensitive U.S. bank stocks rise with major U.S. indexes in morning trading

** U.S. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell on Friday pointed to a possible rate cut at the central bank's September meeting but stopped short of committing

** Rate cuts can ease funding costs for banks, spur loan demand and reduce the risk of borrower defaults

** S&P 500 Banks Index (.SPXBK), which tracks large-cap lenders, last up 1.2%

** KBW Regional Banking Index also up 2.7%

** Rate cuts are especially helpful for regional banks as they rely more on traditional lending, making them sensitive to shifts in borrowing demand, consumer spending power and the health of local businesses

** Powell’s comments open the door to a rate cut at the Fed’s September 16-17 meeting, but also put heavy weight on jobs and inflation reports that will be received before then

data.title: US stocks extend gains after Powell opens door to interest-rate cuts
data.description:
U.S. stock indexes extended gains on Friday after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell pointed to a possible rate cut at the central bank's next policy meeting in his remarks at the Jackson Hole Symposium.

Powell hinted at a possible rate cut at the central bank's September meeting, but stopped short of committing to cutting interest rates in remarks that walked a narrow line acknowledging growing risks to the job market, while also saying risks of higher inflation remained.

At 10:02 a.m. ET, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 670.39 points, or 1.50%, to 45,455.89, the S&P 500 gained 79.25 points, or 1.24%, to 6,449.42, and the Nasdaq Composite gained 261.21 points, or 1.24%, to 21,361.52.

data.title: Powell Says Shifting Balance of Risks May Warrant Adjusting Policy
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chairman Powell noted that inflation has fallen but remains above target, while the labor market is slowing without showing major slack.

Growth has cooled, and new trade and immigration policies are reshaping both supply and demand, raising uncertainty.

Tariffs are lifting prices, but the Fed sees these effects as likely temporary unless they feed into wages or expectations.

The Fed is balancing upside risks to inflation against rising downside risks to jobs.

Policy is still restrictive, but now closer to neutral, allowing flexibility.

Powell stressed that decisions will remain data-driven and not preset.

Importantly, the Fed dropped its 2020 framework’s “makeup” inflation strategy and shifted back to flexible inflation targeting, emphasizing that stable inflation expectations are key.

The revisions clarify that maximum employment can run above estimates without forcing preemptive hikes, but the Fed will act if price stability is threatened.

data.title: Waymo to Begin Testing Its Robotaxis in New York City — Update
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Waymo is coming to New York City.

The self-driving car company, which is owned by Google parent Alphabet, received a permit to begin testing a limited number of autonomous vehicles in parts of Manhattan and Downtown Brooklyn, Mayor Eric Adams and the New York City Transportation Department said Friday.

The vehicles will operate under the nation's strictest autonomous-vehicle safety rules, officials said, including a requirement to have a trained AV specialist behind the wheel at all times.

"These requirements will help ensure that the development of this technology is focused, first and foremost, on the safety of everyone who shares our busy city streets," DOT Commissioner Ydanis Rodriguez said.

New York City has previously played host to self-driving vehicles.

Mobileye, a self-driving car company owned by Intel, began testing its AVs in New York City in 2021, though the company suspended its testing the following year. And Optimus Ride launched a self-driving car service in the Brooklyn Navy Yard in 2019. (The Boston-based start-up discontinued its commercial operations in 2022 after Magna International acquired most of its technology and intellectual property.)

Waymo, whose autonomous taxi services are already available in cities including Phoenix, San Francisco and Los Angeles, has been quickly expanding its footprint. The company said earlier this year that it planned to begin testing its self-driving cars in more than 10 new cities, including Las Vegas and San Diego. More recently, in June, Waymo said it was eyeing its expansion into New York City.

Annabel Chang, the company's head of U.S. state and local public policy, said the company has completed over 10 million rides in five major U.S. cities with a strong safety record.

Waymo will be permitted to test up to eight autonomous vehicles in Manhattan and Downtown Brooklyn until late September 2025. Officials didn't specify where in Manhattan and Downtown Brooklyn the testing will occur, though they noted that the company will be able to apply for an extension once its initial pilot testing period ends.

As part of its permit to operate within New York City, Waymo will be required to coordinate closely with DOT through regular meetings and data reporting.

"New York City is proud to welcome Waymo to test this new technology in Manhattan and Brooklyn, as we know this testing is only the first step in moving our city further into the 21st century," Adams said. "As we continue to implement responsible innovation, we will always prioritize street safety."

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1006 ET - Through the monthly volatility in Canadian retail data it is clear consumer spending growth has slowed amid tariff uncertainty, but hasn't slipped into reverse, CIBC Capital Markets' Andrew Grantham says. Retail sales have been choppy in recent months, rising 1.5% on-month in June after a 1.2% drop in May. And the flash estimate for July points to a 0.8% decline. "This positive yet sluggish trend in demand suggests inflationary pressures should continue to gradually ease, and allow the Bank of Canada to return to interest rate cuts later this year to support the economy," Grantham says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1000 ET - Canadian consumer spending looks to have picked up in June, though it seems momentum was short-lived heading into the second half of the year, BMO Economics' Shelly Kaushik says. "Even so, the big picture suggests consumers are holding up despite ongoing labor market slack and elevated trade uncertainty." Retail sales rose 1.5% on-month in June, a big turnaround from a 1.2% drop in May. All retail sectors saw sales growth, led by clothing and accessories and food and beverage. Still, Kaushik says the flash estimate for July disappointed with a 0.8% decline, suggesting retail volumes could be similarly soft since goods prices were little changed for the month. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0959 ET - The strong rebound in June retail sales in Canada should reassure the Bank of Canada that consumer spending is holding up, although the flash estimate of a drop back in sales in July implies that growth remains muted on net, Capital Economics' Alexandra Brown says. A 1.5% rise on-month was slightly below the flash estimate for a 1.6% advance, while the decline in May sales was revised down 0.1 percentage point to 1.2%. Brown says the result suggest a modest upside risk to Capital's initial estimate that consumption grew by 2% in quarterly annualized terms. Still, the data agency's estimate retail sales fell 0.8% in July is a little worrying, the economist says. It implies retail sales volumes fell by about 1%, which mean consumption growth is likely to remain muted in 3Q, Brown says.(robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0949 ET - Momentum in Canadian consumer spending will wane in the second half of the year as job losses and higher prices from tariffs squeeze disposable income, Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport says. For now, retail sales rebounded 1.5% on-month in June, in line with the economist's forecast, after dropping 1.2% in May. Retail volumes were up a similar 1.5%. Davenport says the data was unequivocally strong, with all nine subsectors tracked rising in nominal and real terms. Still, he says the underlying sales trend remains soft and June's rebound brings volumes back to where they stood in April. Statistics Canada's flash estimate for July suggests nominal sales fell 0.8% on-month. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0859 ET - For the first time since June 2006, outside the height of the pandemic in 2021 anyway, more U.S. residents made trips to Canada than Canadian residents made to the U.S. Data from Statistics Canada shows the number of Canadian-resident return trips from the U.S. was down 28.7% on-year in June, and the number of trips to Canada by U.s. residents declined 5.8%. Canadians have in recent months opted for stay-cations and trips abroad while the trade war with the U.S. drags on. Trips to Canada by overseas residents also faltered in June, slipping 3.9%. But the number of Canadians returning from overseas journeys increased 6.9%, the data agency says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0836 ET - The dollar's gains this week suggest the market is betting that Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell will maintain his cautious stance on interest-rate cuts in Friday's Jackson Hole speech, Rabobank's Jane Foley says in a note. "This view has no doubt found support in last week's release of firmer than expected U.S. producer price inflation data which hinted that trade tariffs were having an impact on producer pricing," she says. Powell's tone will likely determine the dollar's performance heading into the Fed's September meeting, she says. The DXY dollar index trades steady at 98.648 after earlier hitting a one-and-a-half-week high of 98.834. The index has risen 0.8% over the past week, LSEG data show. Powell speaks at 1400 GMT. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0834 ET - Investors are buying up long-term U.S. government debt, sending yields down, ahead of Chair Powell's Jackson Hole speech. Recent data have cooled down expectations of an interest rate cut in September. Powell faces a delicate balancing act and may stay away from issuing clear guidance ahead of August payrolls. Observers expect him to reaffirm the central bank's independence from political pressure. The 10-year is at 4.315%, after settling yesterday at 4.329%. The two-year is little changed, at 3.789%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0728 ET - Sterling could strengthen if the dollar falls and U.K. economic growth improves, Bank of America's Kamal Sharma says in a note. BofA expects the dollar to weaken in anticipation of the Federal Reserve resuming interest-rate cuts even as inflation rises. Regarding U.K. economic growth, the burden of proof will be on third-quarter data for evidence that the slowdown in growth in the second quarter reflected transitory shocks, Sharma says. "We are still of the view that the U.K.'s trade agreements with the U.S. and EU are advantageous for U.K. growth." BofA expects sterling to rise to $1.45 by the fourth quarter, from $1.3413 currently. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0711 ET - Gold futures fall but remain broadly rangebound in muted trading, as the market remains fixed on the outlook for U.S. interest rates. Futures are down 0.3% at $3,372.0 a troy ounce. Investors appear reluctant to take strong positions ahead of a key speech by U.S. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, DHF Capital's Bas Kooijman says in a note. Recent comments from Fed officials suggest caution about moving too quickly when easing monetary policy, though money markets are still broadly betting on a September interest-rate cut, Kooijman writes. At the same time, physical gold demand indicators are showing mixed signals in Asia, contributing to gold's sideways trading. Indian jewelers have cautiously picked up purchases ahead of the festival season, though at much lower than normal levels, he says. Trading is similarly muted in Singapore and Hong Kong, he adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0658 ET - While the German economy's recent past seems gloomy, there could be better times ahead, Berenberg's Salomon Fiedler says in a note. Germany's statistical office revised its estimates of the country's GDP decline in the second quarter to 0.3% from the original estimate of a 0.1% contraction. Weak June data, including for industrial production and construction, had already hinted at a downward revision. But the extent of the change was larger than anticipated, Fiedler says. The strength of the quarterly swing--to a 0.3% contraction from 0.3% growth in 1Q--should now ease, with growth of 0.1% expected in 3Q, he says. The EU-U.S. trade agreement and PMIs point to a modest continuing recovery. Germany's ramp-up in spending after the loosening of its debt brake could also allow GDP growth to accelerate in 2026, he adds. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0647 ET - The global rise in stock prices is likely to continue, UBS's Mark Haefele says in a note. "We expect an economic soft landing, solid corporate earnings, and lower interest rates to support markets over the next 12 months," he says. Corporate earnings continue to beat expectations, justifying the expensive stock valuations, Haefele says. "This suggests to us that markets are not in a bubble." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0654 ET - The downward revision to second-quarter German GDP to a contraction of 0.3% didn't come as much of a surprise given weak industrial output for June and disappointing exports data, HSBC's Anja Sabine Heimann says. The underperformance in investment might reflect a drag from economic uncertainty stemming from tariff tensions, she says. But with a EU-U.S. trade agreement now signed, some uncertainty has been removed. Firms should now be able to plan ahead more confidently again. "We think the second quarter should be the weakest this year," she says. However, tariffs are structurally higher than last year and with a stronger euro against other currencies, exports won't resume their role as Germany's growth engine quite yet--or perhaps ever, Heimann says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)
